data.title: Is $60,000 Bitcoin's Tripwire That Could Unleash A Cascade Of Selloffs?
data.description:
By Krzysztof Kamiński

$60,000 as the market’s flashpoint level

In the Bitcoin (BTC-USD) market, it is becoming increasingly clear that the area around $60,000 serves as both a psychological and technical “front line.” A move below this level could trigger not only a standard correction, but also a sharp rise in volatility driven by the mechanics of derivatives and BTC-collateralized financing. In other words, near $60,000, multiple factors overlap that, if broken to the downside, can turn a price decline into a cascade of forced transactions.

Big bets positioned for a drop below $60,000

One key source of risk is positioning in the options market. On Deribit, there is a strong concentration of puts at the $60,000 strike, with open interest in these instruments reaching roughly $1.24 billion.

This market structure matters not because it “guarantees” a decline, but because as the price approaches $60,000, it can force hedging activity from option writers. As risk rises, put sellers often reduce exposure by selling BTC or futures contracts, increasing selling pressure precisely when the market is most sensitive.

Lower technical support: The 200-week moving average around $58,000

Just below $60,000 there is an additional reference point for some investors: the 200-week moving average, estimated around $58,000. In practice, this means that once $60,000 is broken, the market may quickly test the next technical support. If demand does not respond fast enough, the very fact of the price “sliding” downward can deepen nervousness and accelerate the move.

BTC-collateralized loans: A forced-selling mechanism

Another part of the puzzle is Bitcoin-backed lending. In many financing structures, a drop in price toward specific thresholds triggers automatic actions by the lender—most often the sale of the collateral.

This forced selling acts like fuel for declines: as the price falls, additional supply appears not because investors choose to sell, but because of risk-management procedures. Importantly, this mechanism is particularly dangerous when it overlaps with a derivatives market already burdened with heavy positioning.

Leverage and liquidations: How a drop can feed on itself

In a highly leveraged environment, a price decline quickly worsens collateral metrics in investors’ accounts. When margin no longer meets requirements, positions are forcibly closed, generating another wave of selling and boosting volatility.

In this way, the market can enter a “self-fulfilling” sell-off. Selling causes the price to fall, and falling prices trigger further liquidations. In the past, similar episodes ended with a rapid unwinding of risk as billions of dollars’ worth of bullish bets were wiped out.

Sentiment and scenarios: From a bounce to a move down toward $50,000

Recent weeks suggest that the $60,000 level is being genuinely tested: on February 6, Bitcoin dipped into this zone and then bounced, and on Friday in New York, it was trading around $67,000, while still notably below its earlier peak.

Some market participants emphasize short-term pessimism, visible both in commentary and in hedging structures. In this context, forecast revisions also matter - Standard Chartered lowered its end-2026 target to $100,000, allowing for a drop as low as $50,000 along the way before stabilization. The area around $50,000 is also the next zone of clear put interest.

What this puzzle implies

The $60,000 level is not important solely because it “looks nice” in headlines. Its significance comes from the overlap of several layers of risk: concentrated options positioning, potential hedging activity, threshold levels in BTC-collateralized loans, and the mechanics of leverage and liquidations.

If the price remains above $60,000, the market may function relatively stably despite jittery sentiment. But if there is a sustained breakdown below it, there is a risk that selling pressure will not be a one-off impulse, but rather a sequence of events in which successive market segments force additional trades - raising volatility and deepening the price move.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ARS Pharmaceuticals: Climbing Neffy Sales And Global Expansion Could Re-Rate The Stock
data.description:
ARS Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (SPRY) is a commercial-stage biopharmaceutical company that produces Neffy for type I allergic reactions, including anaphylaxis. This asset is a needle-free intranasal epinephrine dose, which offers a more convenient route of administration than the traditional injectable epinephrine. Neffy has been experiencing an increasing growth in sales since its launch last year due to its comparable efficiency to auto-injectors, with a simpler administration and reduced potential for user error. SPRY’s pipeline also includes ARS-2, which is an intranasal epinephrine program for chronic spontaneous urticaria acute flares. This candidate is now in Phase 2b, and SPRY plans to release its data readout by 1H2026.

Neffy And Epinephrine

ARS Pharmaceuticals, Inc. is a biopharmaceutical company that develops needle-free products to treat severe allergic reactions. This means that one of its key addressable markets is in treating anaphylaxis. SPRY was founded back in 2015 and is currently headquartered in San Diego, California. I previously covered SPRY in February last year, and since then, the stock has seen considerable price swings. Yet, as of the time of this writing, SPRY is down about 30.9%, so I thought it was worthwhile updating my analysis on this name.

As a quick recap, SPRY’s portfolio contains an epinephrine spray called Neffy. This is SPRY’s FDA-approved intranasal medication indicated for type I allergic reactions that are fast and can escalate to life-threatening emergencies. Fortunately, SPRY has positioned its nasal spray as a convenient solution, especially compared to other traditional epinephrine auto-injectors that can be much more cumbersome during emergencies.

It’s also worth highlighting that this product was launched in May 2025. As of Q3 2025, Neffy’s quarterly sales reached $31.3 million, which is impressive given that it only did $7.8 million in revenues in Q1 2025. In fact, SPRY believes Neffy has a potential multi-billion-dollar opportunity in the US market alone, mostly thanks to its needle-free administration. Essentially, the bull case with this asset is its more convenient administration method, which could be a decisive factor for gaining market share with more units consumed per patient. SPRY says that there is a segment of patients (around 13.5 million) who are diagnosed but not prescribed, which could add $7 billion in potential sales expansion.

Moreover, management mentioned that people with severe allergies don’t use injectable epinephrine correctly. After all, its application is difficult, generally speaking, and especially during moments that cause panic, fear, or confusion. In those scenarios, it’s unrealistic to expect everyday patients (or friends/family) to adequately administer injections, which often cause failures. That’s why a needle-free nasal option, like Neffy, should improve usability, producing better results and larger adoption. And, in my view, it’s reasonable to expect such a solution to also capture market share from injectables, which further increases Neffy’s market opportunity.

On top of that, SPRY is marketing Neffy with a direct-to-consumer (DTC) campaign that is directed to patients themselves. The ads then redirect patients interested in Neffy to SPRY’s webpage so they can directly buy the medication themselves. This offer also has a $0 co-pay for commercially insured individuals, which is another factor that helps SPRY convert more sales with minimal cost and hassle. And more importantly, it bypasses advertising to healthcare providers that may undervalue the importance of Neffy’s convenient delivery mechanism.

Also, note that in terms of effectiveness, SPRY claims that in real-life anaphylaxis events, a single dose of Neffy has the same rate of success as a single dose of epinephrine injection. Concretely, Neffy’s success rate is around 89.2% based on 545 patients in the real-world dataset, which is actually slightly better than the injections’ historical success rate of 88.9%. SPRY intends to publish this data to bolster confidence in Neffy among healthcare professionals and patients alike, which will likely increase uptake as well.

Commercial Prospects

Moreover, SPRY’s near-term commercial efforts are now in the US. But, they’re also partnered through regional licensing deals that help distribute Neffy across the rest of the world using regional licensing deals. Neffy is approved in the US, Europe, the United Kingdom, China, Australia, and Japan. It’s also under review in Canada and New Zealand. In the US, Neffy was approved via a 505(b)(2) NDA, which is used whenever a new product results in a meaningful change of some kind.

Even though the active ingredient in Neffy is epinephrine (used for decades with auto-injectors), Neffy is inherently differentiated due to its administration route and delivery device. With that in mind, so far Neffy’s international licenses have produced $570 million in upfront payments, milestones, and royalties. SPRY projects this asset can reach peak sales of around $425 million across ex-US territories where ALK-Abelló (ALK_B) has commercialization rights. Those rights include Europe, Canada, and the United Kingdom, among others. Alfresa Pharma is licensed for Japan, and Pediatrix Therapeutics for China.

Aside from that, SPRY is also working on a Phase 2b candidate called ARS-2. This is intranasal epinephrine for chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU) acute flares. This product has the same molecule and administration route as Neffy, but it’s repurposed for CSU. That means, SPRY has to work out a different dosing and frequency schedule, which technically makes ARS-2 a new program. SPRY plans to release its 2b data by 1H2026. But this could be another important catalyst for SPRY because (if eventually approved), it could unlock an estimated TAM of 2 million diagnosed CSU patients in the US alone. Out of those 2 million patients, 1 million are already prescribed some kind of medication. In other words, this indication could be another major value vertical opportunity estimated at roughly $2 billion in peak net sales potential.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, SPRY trades at a $939.1 million market cap. Their latest 10-Q from Q3 2025 shows its balance sheet holds $59.6 million in cash and $228.7 million in short-term investments. This amounts to approximately $288.3 million in liquid assets to fund its R&D and operations, against $96.2 million in net term loans. Their book value reached $147.7 million, which would imply an inflated P/B of around 6.4. For comparison, their sector’s median P/B is closer to 2.8, suggesting SPRY might be at a premium valuation already.

However, using my figures, I estimate SPRY’s EV at approximately $806.6 million. And, according to Seeking Alpha’s dashboard on SPRY, the company is projected to generate $172.7 million in sales this year. That prices SPRY at a more manageable EV/S of 4.7 relative to its peers’ median EV/S of 3.7. But, after you consider SPRY’s sizeable TAMs across anaphylaxis and potentially CSU (if their Phase 2b yields promising data and is eventually approved), then the stock seems quite compelling at these levels. After all, that implies there’s still a lot of room for future growth, which should continue to outpace the sector’s topline growth rates of only 6.6%.

Having said that, it seems that this aggressive growth strategy hasn’t turned them cash flow positive so far. This is to be expected to some extent, especially during a ramp-up period. But for Q3 alone, I estimate SPRY burned through approximately $47.2 million. Note that I got that figure by simply adding its quarterly cash flows from operations and CAPEX. Yet, that number implies a yearly burn rate of around $188.8 million, which could quickly eat through SPRY’s cash reserves.

Still, for now, these figures imply that SPRY’s cash runway is around 1.5 years, which seems manageable in my view. But the acid test will come after we see their 2026 revenue growth materialize and see how their margins perform at that scale. Until then, this remains a risk that’s worth discounting, so bear that in mind. And naturally, it’s always possible that their Phase 2b on CSU fails. Thus, any negative headlines like that could easily compress the stock’s multiples even further as well.

Conclusion: In The “Buy” Zone Again

Overall, SPRY continues to execute on its growth strategy with Neffy. I believe their prospects with this IP are fantastic in the long run, and in that sense, its valuation appears compelling at these levels. This is especially true since the stock has already pulled back around 52% from its 2025 highs, which leads me to believe that a much steeper decline at this point is less likely. On the other hand, SPRY has a couple of important upcoming growth catalysts for anaphylaxis and CSU in 2026. Thus, on balance, I feel it’s again a great time to be bullish on the stock. Just make sure you keep your position sizing under control until we see their Phase 2b data and how margins evolve at a larger scale.

data.title: Inflation Is Cooling: Jan 2026 Increase Below Seasonal Norms
data.description:
By Bryan Cutsinger

Shelter and energy costs led the deceleration. While inflation remains above the Fed’s target, the monthly price growth neared a multi-year low.

Inflation cooled more than expected in January, the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) reported on Friday. The Consumer Price Index (CPI) rose 0.2 percent last month, down from 0.3 percent in December. On a year-over-year basis, headline inflation fell from 2.7 percent in December 2025 to 2.4 percent in January 2026 — the lowest reading since May 2025.

Core inflation, which excludes volatile food and energy prices, rose 0.3 percent in January, up from 0.2 percent in December. It eased to 2.5 percent on a year-over-year basis, down from 2.6 percent in the prior month. The January reading marks the slowest annual pace for core CPI since March 2021.

The latest inflation data are especially encouraging when viewed against historical patterns. Research from the Federal Reserve Bank of Boston shows that January inflation has consistently run higher than other months over the past quarter-century, owing to residual seasonality, the tendency for firms to change prices at the start of the year, and compositional effects in sectors that typically adjust prices in January. That January 2026 came in at just 0.2 percent (below the historical January average), suggesting that underlying inflation pressures are genuinely moderating.

The moderation in headline inflation was driven primarily by energy prices, which fell 1.5 percent in January. Gasoline prices declined, and utility costs moderated. Food prices rose a modest 0.2 percent, with food at home and food away from home both posting smaller increases than in recent months.

Shelter costs, which account for roughly one-third of the index, rose 0.2 percent - a notable deceleration from the 0.4 percent increase in December. The slower pace of shelter inflation is welcome news, as this category has been one of the most persistent sources of upward pressure on prices over the past several years.

Other components of the index showed mixed results. Airline fares surged 6.5 percent in January, continuing their volatile pattern. Appliance prices also surged in January, rising 4.4 percent. Apparel prices rose, while used vehicle prices fell 1.8 percent. Medical care services increased 0.4 percent.

While the year-over-year figures show continued disinflation, the recent three-month trend tells a more nuanced story. Inflation averaged 0.2 percent per month in November (0.2 percent, estimated), December (0.3 percent), and January (0.2 percent) - equivalent to a roughly 2.9 percent annual rate. Core prices averaged 0.2 percent monthly over the same period, also equivalent to a 2.9 percent annual rate. Both measures suggest inflation continues to exceed the Fed’s two-percent target.

Although the Federal Reserve officially targets the personal consumption expenditures price index (PCEPI), CPI data remain a timely and relevant gauge for policymakers. The two measures generally track one another closely, though CPI tends to run somewhat higher than PCE inflation.

Historically, the gap between year-over-year core CPI and core PCE has averaged around 0.3 to 0.4 percentage points, meaning that January’s 2.5 percent core CPI reading likely translates to core PCE inflation in the range of 2.1 to 2.2 percent - very close to the Fed’s two-percent target.

That makes the latest CPI readings particularly encouraging for policymakers as they assess the stance of policy. That said, current expectations of PCE inflation are higher than CPI inflation, potentially because measurement disruptions related to last fall’s government shutdown may have temporarily biased CPI readings downward.

Financial markets seem to have interpreted the latest inflation data as a sign that the FOMC will continue cutting its federal funds rate target later this year. According to the CME Group’s FedWatch tool, markets continue to expect the Fed to hold rates steady at its March meeting.

However, the probability of a rate cut by June rose sharply to approximately 83 percent following the release - a dramatic reversal from earlier in the week, when a strong jobs report had pushed odds of a June cut below 50 percent. The shift reflects renewed confidence that inflation is moving closer to target even as the labor market remains resilient.

The January CPI report offers encouraging signs that inflation is approaching the Fed’s two-percent target. The sharp decline in energy prices and the deceleration in shelter costs are particularly welcome developments.

While some uncertainty remains - particularly given methodological adjustments made to account for missing October 2025 data - the trend is moving in the right direction. Whether policymakers view current rates as neutral or mildly restrictive, the improving inflation picture provides room for the Fed to continue its gradual normalization process later this year without risking a resurgence in price pressures.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ATS Corporation: Positioned For Margin Expansion And Steady Organic Growth
data.description:
Introduction

It’s been a while since my last piece on ATS Corporation (ATS:CA)(ATS) back in August 2024. Since then, the company has shown impressive organic growth despite a softer macro environment. While shares have lagged the S&P 500’s return of 26% with a return of 16% since my last update, I think shares are still worthy of a ‘buy’ given the renewed focus on margin expansion going forward.

Recent Results

ATS reported strong results for its Q3’26 results with a beat on both the top and bottom line. During the quarter, ATS put up revenues of $761 million, which came in 16.7% higher compared to last year and was $38 million ahead of consensus estimates. Most of this was organic growth (12.6%), with a smaller (4.1%) lift from FX translation.

When looking at the results by segment, Life Sciences revenues, the company’s largest segment at 51% of total revenues, was up 4% compared to last year, and the company’s backlog is full of radiopharma and GLP-1 auto-injector programs. Total company order bookings reached $821 million, up nearly 12% sequentially despite a 7% year on year decline tied to normalized Transportation activity and lapping prior large enterprise wins. That puts the company’s book-to-bill ratio at 1.06:1 and the backlog at $2.05 billion, roughly unchanged from last year. When looking at the backlog composition below, there’s been a dip in Life Sciences with record numbers in Energy and Consumer Products.

With a new CEO that took the helm in January who has a focus on profitability, I’d expect ATS to continue making more progress on its margins. This quarter, gross margins dipped 111 bps to 29.6%, a move I view as largely attributable to program mix timing rather than any structural issue. The reason for this was there was a lot more lower-margin nuclear refurbishment work ramping up while still contributing positively to the bottom line. To illustrate that, operating earnings were 21.6% higher compared to last year at $79.9 million, and that was due to modest SG&A growth (mostly on FX) and some labor cost increases, but overall the quarter aligned with expectations and reinforced the company's ability to drive bottom-line leverage from volume gains.

From a balance sheet perspective, ATS continued to make measured progress toward greater efficiency and financial flexibility. With cash of $263 million and debt of $1.36 billion against EBITDA of $408 million, net debt to EBITDA sits at 3x, at the higher end of the targeted 2.0-3.0x range. Cash flows from operations were $115 million, supporting a sequential improvement in noncash working capital to 16.4% of revenues, and that figure more than covers this quarter's capex of $17 million. With full year capex of $80 million +/- $10 million, the company’s free cash flow generation is strong. As leverage moderates further, the company will likely step up its M&A initiatives as it has historically done in the past.

ATS is well positioned longer term

ATS took the Q4 conference call as an opportunity to provide guidance for Q4. Management’s expectation is for revenues to fall in the range of $710-750 million, which points to a slight sequential softening from Q3’s $761 million but still would put it on pace for a high-single digit growth quarter year over year. In my view, this reflects disciplined execution against the $2.1 billion backlog, which remains well diversified and supportive of steady conversion into revenue over the near term. Some of the recent structural changes like embedding Services within operating units, should help cushion any lumpiness, but management also acknowledged some reinvestment of restructuring savings into growth areas. That could put a bit of near-term pressure on margins as those flow through more meaningfully into FY’27.

In the short term, I believe the coming quarters look pretty constructive. Life Sciences' still has a $1.1 billion backlog and ongoing demand in radiopharma and GLP-1 scaling, and the Energy’s segment record backlog puts ATS in a position to drive more diversified growth from its operating units. While auto-injector orders could moderate a little on customer buying cycles and Transportation stays targeted rather than expansive, I’d say the healthy funnels in Consumer Products, Food & Beverage, and other verticals should sustain a book-to-bill above 1 and support mid-single-digit organic growth. While it might not be the continued 8% we saw this quarter, I’d still have confidence in the business model given that the bottom line is unlikely to be impacted much by tariffs since the company essentially runs on a decentralized model.

Longer term, I see a path to 30%+ gross margins. The reason for that is that under the new CEO Doug Wright, he did emphasize his focus on unlocking margin expansion that “has not been realized” and the team's acknowledgment that “we need to do better” through sharper deployment of the ATS Business Model tools, commercial discipline, and a higher aftermarket mix. This tells me the push toward advanced applications in Life Sciences and nuclear new builds, including “a full handful of SMR customers in the early-stage activities,” positions ATS to capture more value from technically complex projects. With leverage now at the top of the targeted range, management’s comment that investors should “expect us to favor deploying capital toward M&A going forward” suggests inorganic opportunities will play a larger role in complementing organic efforts. As ATS becomes much more like the acquisitive company it was of the past, the key will be becoming a more diverse company that has a better profitability profile.

Risks to consider

It’s important to realize that even though the company has done a better job of diversifying its end markets, most of these end markets are in cyclical industries such as industrial automation, electric vehicles, and life sciences. That makes the company vulnerable to downturns in capital spending. Its reliance on large, project-based contracts increases revenue volatility (we see this regularly show up in large backlog fluctuations), and there’s likely some customer concentration risk (ATS doesn’t usually disclose its customer concentration mix).

Another risk would be the macroeconomic and geopolitical factors that have the potential to disrupt capital deployment in ATS's key end markets. While these are risks that are hard to hedge against, it’s easy to see how things like potential trade policy changes and supply chain disruptions are critical barriers to large projects, which ATS ultimately benefits from. As an example, if we were to see slower than anticipated nuclear new-build progress because of regulatory hurdles (or maybe even none at all), that would have a negative impact on the company’s business results. The fact that the company is diversified throughout its end markets helps, but this is certainly a risk to be aware of.

Decent valuation makes for a good entry point

On valuation, I think ATS trades inexpensively relative to peers and is cheap for its future growth expectations. At 12.1x EV/EBITDA and 20.3x P/E, the company trades below a market multiple and is at a near 2-turn discount to EV/EBITDA and a 3-turn discount to peers, as shown below (source: Bloomberg).

For the growth ahead, analysts see 17% top line growth in FY’26 followed by 5% growth in each of FY’27 and FY’28 with gross margins expanding to 30% over two years. With EPS expectations of $2.13 in FY’27, the company isn’t expensive on forward multiples either (19.6x forward earnings).

Conclusion

There were a lot of positives in ATS’ latest quarter with strong organic growth, a healthy backlog, and improving operating leverage despite some segment mix headwinds. With a diversified $2+ billion backlog, expanding exposure to higher-value applications in Life Sciences and Energy, and a push toward margin expansion under the new CEO, I think there’s a credible path to sustained mid-single-digit organic growth and 30%+ gross margins over time. While cyclical end markets and project timing create some lumpiness in the numbers, I think this is still a business that can generate growing free cash flow over time, do so with minimal leverage, and continue to make acquisitions at reasonable multiples. I maintain my ‘buy’ rating.

data.title: This Week's Market Wrap: AI Moving Fast And Breaking Things
data.description:
By Ryan J. Puplava

AI Capex Supercycle Collides With Software and Hardware Reality

Hyperscaler and platform spending remained the backbone of bullish sentiment. Amazon’s (AMZN) staggering $200 billion 2026 capex plan underscored that cloud leaders are spending aggressively to secure compute dominance, even at the expense of near-term profitability.

Meta Platforms (META) added fuel to that narrative, breaking ground on a $10+ billion AI data center in Indiana capable of delivering one gigawatt of compute capacity. Meanwhile, private capital markets validated the demand backdrop: Anthropic’s (ANTHRO) reported $20 billion funding round at a $350 billion valuation highlighted that frontier model developers still have deep access to capital.

The downstream effects were visible across infrastructure suppliers. Vertiv (VRT) surged after reporting organic order growth exceeding 250% year over year, while Cloudflare (NET) rallied on strong forward guidance tied to AI network demand.

Power and grid exposure remained another critical lever. American Electric Power (AEP) cited surging electricity commitments tied to data center load, reinforcing the structural power-demand thesis supporting utilities and gas turbine manufacturers like GE Vernova (GEV).

Yet even as AI infrastructure spending accelerated, cracks widened elsewhere. Software multiples came under renewed pressure, culminating in Thursday’s sharp selloff after AppLovin (APP) delivered a beat-and-raise quarter - only to plunge nearly 20%. The reaction reinforced a key market anxiety: strong earnings are no longer enough if investors believe AI will erode long-term pricing power.

Cisco’s (CSCO) results crystallized the hardware side of that tension. Despite topping estimates, the company warned that rising high-bandwidth memory costs would compress margins. That commentary weighed on peers, including Dell (DELL) and Apple (AAPL) and dragged semiconductor sentiment lower. Samsung’s (SSNLF) announcement that it had begun mass production of next-generation HBM4 chips highlighted the bottleneck: AI demand remains insatiable, but supply chain cost pressures are intensifying.

Even industrial and logistics firms were pulled into the disruption narrative. Courier stocks such as C.H. Robinson (CHRW) and Expeditors International (EXPD) sold off sharply amid fears that AI-driven supply chain optimization could disintermediate traditional freight brokers.

The result was a market struggling to price AI’s duality: explosive demand creation alongside margin compression and product obsolescence.

Investment Implication: Relative performance across the AI ecosystem may continue to differentiate between infrastructure and semiconductor providers and certain application-layer software companies, as generative AI adoption evolves.

Macro Crosscurrents Reprice the Fed Path

Economic data this week delivered a classic late-cycle paradox: growth resilience paired with incremental disinflation. That combination forced markets to recalibrate the timing and magnitude of future Federal Reserve rate cuts rather than the direction of policy itself.

The January Employment Situation Report surprised materially to the upside, with 130,000 payrolls added versus expectations for roughly 68,000. Private payrolls and wage growth also exceeded forecasts, while unemployment dipped to 4.3%. For equity markets, the signal was mixed. Strong labor demand reinforced the durability of the U.S. expansion but simultaneously reduced urgency for near-term policy easing.

Rate-cut probabilities adjusted quickly. Expectations for a June Fed cut fell from above 75% to near 60% following the release. Treasury yields moved accordingly, with the 2-year rising to 3.51% intraday before stabilizing.

Yet inflation data later in the week softened the hawkish impulse. January CPI rose 0.2% month over month, below consensus expectations, while core CPI matched forecasts and showed continued year-over-year disinflation. Markets interpreted the report as evidence that price pressures continue to moderate even as growth holds above trend.

Additional data points filled in the macro mosaic. The Employment Cost Index showed wage pressures cooling on a year-over-year basis. Retail sales, however, were flat in December, raising concerns about consumer fatigue following the holiday season. Existing home sales missed expectations as supply constraints kept affordability tight despite lower mortgage rates.

Fiscal dynamics also entered the conversation. The Treasury reported a January deficit of $94.6 billion, far narrower than anticipated, aided in part by customs-duty collections. Still, rising interest expense highlighted the structural burden of elevated debt issuance.

Markets oscillated accordingly. Strong growth data pressured mega-cap valuations via higher real-rate assumptions, while cooler inflation prints supported defensive and rate-sensitive sectors like utilities and real estate on Friday.

The week’s price action reflected that tension. Utilities outperformed materially, while housing stocks caught a bid after the House passed legislation aimed at boosting supply. Meanwhile, cyclical leadership faltered whenever yields pushed higher.

Investment Implication: Economic resilience has supported cyclical sectors, while moderating inflation and potential Fed easing may influence the outlook for more interest-rate-sensitive areas such as utilities, REITs, and high-quality bonds.

Crypto Volatility and Retail Speculation Feedback Loops

Digital assets once again proved their ability to transmit volatility across asset classes. Bitcoin’s (BTC-USD) sharp swings reverberated through fintech equities, retail brokerage platforms, and broader risk sentiment.

The week began with crypto stabilizing after a violent drawdown that nearly pushed Bitcoin below $60,000, far removed from its record highs above $126,000 last October. The selloff had been exacerbated by forced liquidations tied to volatility in tech equities and precious metals - particularly silver - highlighting the increasingly interconnected nature of speculative positioning.

Crypto-linked equities reflected that turbulence. Coinbase (COIN) reported quarterly revenue that slightly missed expectations and posted a $667 million net loss, driven in part by markdowns on its crypto asset portfolio and strategic investments. Despite the earnings miss, the stock staged a sharp rebound later in the week as Bitcoin prices recovered, illustrating its high beta to the underlying asset rather than fundamentals alone.

Robinhood (HOOD) provided another window into retail trading dynamics. Shares dropped sharply after missing revenue expectations, with management pointing to weaker crypto activity as a headwind. The stock’s continued post-earnings slide dragged on financials sentiment more broadly.

Yet speculative appetite was far from extinguished. Coinbase’s late-week rally, alongside rebounds in courier stocks and software names, signaled that traders were willing to re-engage risk when macro and liquidity signals stabilized.

Corporate strategy also reflected crypto’s evolving competitive landscape. DraftKings (DKNG) plunged after issuing weak guidance and warning that prediction markets - often blockchain-enabled - were emerging as competitive threats to traditional sports betting platforms.

The interplay between crypto prices and equity flows remains critical. When digital assets rally, they inject liquidity into retail trading ecosystems, boosting volumes for exchanges and brokers. When they fall, the reverse liquidity drain can tighten financial conditions at the margin.

Investment Implication: Crypto-exposed equities have exhibited sensitivity to broader liquidity conditions and Bitcoin price movements, which may serve as indicators of shifting retail risk appetite.

Chart of the Week

The US dollar has been in the news this week as articles began floating that Russia may be close to an economic deal with President Trump. The deal includes seven areas, including “fossil fuels, natural gas, offshore oil, and critical raw materials,” according to the Economic Times, and includes the possible return for Russia to the US dollar settlement system.

Structurally, the dollar has fallen below a hard support for the dollar above 100 on the US Dollar Index in 2025. It currently rests at another mid-90s level above 96. The next support, should the 2025 downtrend persist, is 90. A move down to 90 in 2026 would continue to support higher commodity prices we’ve been seeing in precious metals, basic metals, and recently energy prices.

Source: StockCharts, Ryan Puplava, CMT® CTS™ CES™

Bottom Line

This week’s market narrative revolved around three intersecting forces: the scale of AI capital investment, macroeconomic recalibration of Fed policy, and crypto-driven liquidity swings. Together, they produced choppy index performance but rich cross-asset signals.

AI remains the dominant secular driver, but leadership is narrowing toward infrastructure and energy beneficiaries. Economic data continues to validate expansion while complicating the easing timeline. And crypto volatility is amplifying retail participation cycles rather than fading from relevance.

For portfolio construction, the message is one of selectivity rather than broad beta exposure - favor structural winners with pricing power and capital-cycle leverage while remaining tactical in speculative and liquidity-sensitive segments.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Sendas Distribuidora: Deleveraging Delivered, Awaiting The Macro Turn
data.description:
In a previous work, my bullish call on Sendas Distribuidora (ASAIY) (but under the trade name Assaí) was based on three pillars.

The first was about a turning point in the Brazilian macro. Interest rates (Selic) are stuck at 15%, with the market beginning to price the end of the tightening cycle, with inflation converging within the margin limit stipulated by the Central Bank of Brazil—3% with ± 1.5% tolerance—and the historically strong negative correlation between ASAIY ADR and interest rates in the country.

The second pillar had to do with deleveraging as a catalyst. Assaí's net debt had fallen from ~4.7x at the peak of its leverage in 2023 to 3.17x in the last earnings print. The management team had set an explicit target of 2.6x by 4Q25, with 90% of EBITDA generated being directed to debt reduction, in addition to CapEx also being reduced with the maturation of the stores—officially welcoming the thesis for financial normalization and bidding farewell to the appeal of “aggressive growth”.

The third and final pillar was about ASAIY's valuation anchored in that same debt reduction. I argued back in September last year that there could be significant upside only via net debt reduction, as the multiple could expand with interest expenses—which comprised ~4% of total revenues—moderating. In other words, the broad scenario for Assaí was equity re-rate via capital structure improvement.

A couple of quarters have passed since then, so let's see where the thesis stands and whether these pillars have materialized to any extent.

Execution Intact Amid Deflationary Pressures

As the Brazilian cash and carry company reported its 4Q25 figures, I believe that the main point about Assaí's execution thesis was the achievement of the 2.6x leverage target. And yes, the company closed the quarter with 2.56x net debt/EBITDA (pre-IFRS).

Now, how does a company achieve this in a scenario where it has a nominal cost of debt of 16.3% per year? The answer would be the equation of: (1) strong cash generation; added to (2) capital discipline and lower CapEx needs; generating (3) a debt reduction faster than EBITDA fell.

Assaí generated R$2.8 billion in free cash flow in 2025, equivalent to roughly 84% EBITDA-to-cash conversion. This amount allowed the company to reduce net debt by R$1.2 billion during the year, even while net financial expenses increased to R$584 million in 4Q25 versus R$400 million in 4Q24.

Of course, financial expenses impact net income—not EBITDA. And as leverage is measured against EBITDA, the key driver was debt reduction funded by operational cash flow rather than lower interest costs.

As a result, a lower CapEx base of R$533 million, less than the same period last year, and reduced acquisition-related spending of a massive ~R$880 million helped accelerate deleveraging, while adjusted EBITDA reached R$3.7 billion (+16.8% QoQ; +3% YoY).

But going further, also very relevant in Assaí's latest print was the growth in revenues and same-store sales (volume) numbers.

Assaí posted consolidated net revenue of R$20.8 billion (+9.7% QoQ; +3.1% YoY). However, the headline 3% growth in top line does not tell the whole story. As the earnings call details, 4Q25 was marked by simultaneous deflation in multiple commodities relevant to the core of cash & carry—something somewhat unusual in historical terms. Staple items in the low-income Brazilian consumer basket saw significant price declines, such as rice, milk, and sugar plummeting by -37%, -16%, and -11%.

The key point here is that the volume sold in kilos and tons is growing. But what remained below inflation (measured by the IPCA, which came in at 4.4%) was nominal growth, precisely because Assaí is highly exposed to these categories of low-value addition and strong weight in the mix—i.e., Assaí sales rose, but earned less than inflation would suggest.

And there lies the explanation why same-store sales grew only 2.6% per year—even with traffic remaining stable at approximately 40 million customers per month. So the issue was much more about a mix pressured by deflation and trade-down than empty stores.

Another very relevant point for understanding Assaí's momentum (and the cash & carry scenario in Brazil) was the so-called "K-effect," introduced by the company's own management team. In this case, formats exposed to higher-income customers grew +4%, while formats more dependent on low income showed a decline of close to -9%. When we synchronize this with the macro backdrop in Brazil, it is easier to understand this bifurcation. According to data cited by Assaí's management team, Brazil has approximately R$7 trillion allocated to fixed income, while approximately R$4 trillion is in household debt. In other words, part of the population has benefited from high borrowing costs, while another part suffers from high interest rates and indebtedness.

In the case of Assaí, the side effect is a trade-down within customers' shopping carts (opting for cheaper brands), greater price sensitivity, and nominal pressure on same-store sales even with resilient volume. And for the B2B segment—which represents a considerable share of sales—there is still the component of greater sensitivity to the deflationary environment as small retailers reduce inventory replenishment to avoid losses from falling prices—which generates a cautious purchasing dynamic even without structural deterioration in final demand.

How Should Investors View ASAIY From Here?

Arguably, weaker nominal revenue growth does not invalidate Assai1's thesis, but rather qualifies it. If the pressure came mainly from price and mix rather than traffic or volume, then a normalization of commodities could re-accelerate nominal growth without the need for a demand "miracle."

Assaí recognizes that prices such as R$12 (~US$2) for rice are not structurally sustainable. If there is a partial recovery of these prices, the arithmetic effect on revenue tends to be immediate.

Therefore, the latest earnings print shows that Assaí relied on (1) resilient volume; (2) defensive traffic; (3) mix pressured by deflation; and (4) consumption bifurcation.

Within the broader context of the thesis—delivered deleveraging and maintained capital discipline—I see the revenue dynamics as more of a temporary headwind than a sign of structural deterioration.

All of this is important for how to look through the lens of valuation. I believe that starting in 4Q25, Assaí's valuation narrative may begin to detach itself from the defensive interest rate cut and move closer to a story of stock re-rating, for three reasons that are directly connected to operating metrics and the macro scenario itself.

In this case, if the main valuation driver of the last cycle was the disciplined execution of the deleveraging plan, based on the current EV/EBITDA of 6.4x, I would say that recent movements have already taken Assaí's ADR out of the "multiple distress" of 4.5x, but it is still far from returning to the average multiple of 8x. So here, I believe the interpretation is that deleveraging has been delivered, but the multiple has not yet normalized.

But I also would not expect the multiple to converge to 8x again with a macro scenario of high interest rates in Brazil and Assaí's nominal top line growth slowing down—even though deflation appears to be cyclical. As the Selic rate was kept at 15% at the end of January (even with inflation very close to the target), a cut seems inevitable at the next meetings of Brazil's monetary policy committee (Copom)—the next one being scheduled for March 18.

Giving a brief overview of why this is the scenario being priced in the Brazilian macro, the main reasons for believing in a cut in March can be explained by: (1) cumulative annual inflation hovering around ~4.3%–4.5% within the tolerance band, with agricultural commodities in deflation and services losing marginal traction—the ex-ante real interest rate is above 10%; (2) the output gap is widening with low-income consumption in Brazil under pressure (as Assaí itself showed in the call); and (3) the unemployment rate of ~7.5%-7.8% is still historically low but has stopped falling.

I believe there is a high probability that Copom will cut the Selic rate in March by 25 bps to 50 bps, which should ease Assaí's implied cost of capital and may support an expansion of multiples closer to the historical average (~8x)—although part of this assumption is already being priced in, but not entirely, in my view.

Risks and Final Remarks

The primary risk for ASAIY remains macroeconomic. If Brazil's central bank chooses to keep the Selic rate high for longer—whether due to fiscal deterioration, reanchoring of expectations, or exchange rate shocks—the cost of capital would remain under pressure and the normalization of multiples could take longer than the market anticipates. The persistence of the K-effect with structurally weak low income is also a considerable risk factor, despite indications that it is somewhat cyclical.

That said, I believe that 4Q25 marks an important turning point in Assaí's thesis, as deleveraging was successfully delivered, while cash generation proved robust under 16% nominal interest rates, and volume performed defensively despite price compression. With an imminent turnaround in the Brazilian macro environment, the narrative will tend to shift from "survival" equity to "normalization," and with that, I believe investors will be able to target multiples more in line with the median, which would be a relevant upside given the current asymmetry. Therefore, I reiterate the Buy rating on ASAIY.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Antero Midstream: Potential For $1.3 Billion In 2027 Adjusted EBITDA (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Antero Midstream (AM) ended 2025 with adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow after dividends that were around the high end of its revised guidance (which was an improvement over its original guidance). It expects its 2026 adjusted EBITDA growth to be driven by its HG Energy acquisition, but also suggests high-single-digit adjusted EBITDA growth in 2027. This would put its 2027 adjusted EBITDA around $1.3 billion, which I believe would support an increased $0.30 per share quarterly dividend (while keeping its free cash flow after dividends above $300 million).

I am increasing my estimate of Antero Midstream's value to $21.20 per share, based on that 2027 outlook and a 50/50 chance of winning $300+ million in the Veolia lawsuit. With Antero Midstream trading around that, I am moving back to a "Neutral" outlook on it.

Q4 2025 Results

Antero Midstream's recent results have been relatively consistent and at the higher end of expectations. Its low-pressure gathering and compression volumes were unchanged in Q4 2025 compared to Q3 2025, while its high-pressure gathering volumes were up 1% quarter-over-quarter.

This contributed to Antero Midstream generating $285 million in adjusted EBITDA in Q4 2025, which was a slight increase over its $281 million in adjusted EBITDA in Q3 2025 and its $284 million in adjusted EBITDA in Q2 2025.

This puts it at $1.125 billion in adjusted EBITDA for the full year, towards the high end of its revised guidance for $1.09 billion to $1.13 billion in adjusted EBITDA. Antero's 2025 guidance for adjusted EBITDA was increased by $10 million in Q2 2025.

Antero Midstream reported $86 million in free cash flow after dividends in Q4 2025. This allowed it to reduce its net debt to slightly below $3 billion at the end of 2025 as well as continue repurchasing shares. Antero Midstream repurchased 2.7 million shares for $48 million in Q4 2025, and had $336 million in remaining capacity under its share repurchase program at the end of 2025.

Antero Midstream ended up with $325 million in free cash flow after dividends for the full year, which was at the high end of its revised guidance for $275 million to $325 million in free cash flow after dividends. This revised guidance was also a $25 million increase over its original guidance for free cash flow after dividends.

2026 Guidance

Antero Midstream provided its 2026 guidance, which includes the impact of its HG Energy acquisition closing in early February and its Ohio Utica Shale divestiture occurring in late February.

I would say that its guidance looks to be fairly conservative. Antero Midstream expects $1.21 billion in 2026 adjusted EBITDA at its guidance midpoint, which is an 8% increase compared to 2025. That guidance would suggest roughly flat adjusted EBITDA growth from its legacy assets, though. If it can generate around 2% organic adjusted EBITDA growth instead, it would end up at the high end of its 2026 guidance.

In $ Millions 2026 Guidance
Adjusted EBITDA $1,210
Capital Expenditures $205
Interest $220
Cash Taxes $0
FCF Before Dividends $785
Dividends $425
FCF After Dividends $360

Antero Midstream also expects $360 million in free cash flow after dividends in 2026 at its guidance midpoint. This is an 11% increase compared to 2025. This number is incrementally increased by Antero Midstream's share repurchases reducing its dividend payments.

Potential 2027 Outlook

Antero Midstream expects that Antero Resources continuing to run three rigs and two completion crews will result in high-single digit adjusted EBITDA growth for Antero Midstream in 2027. Antero Midstream would also benefit slightly from a full year of contributions from the HG Energy assets.

Thus Antero Midstream could see around $1.3 billion in 2027 adjusted EBITDA. I can envision Antero Midstream increasing its dividend with that amount of projected adjusted EBITDA.

A quarterly dividend of $0.30 per share (close to the $0.3075 per quarter share dividend it had between mid-2019 to early 2021) would then result in Antero Midstream having around $320 million in free cash flow after dividends. This is similar to its 2025 free cash flow after dividends.

Notes On Valuation

I estimated Antero Midstream's value at approximately $20.20 per share after its HG Energy acquisition. I am increasing that estimate by $1 per share to $21.20 per share, which reflects Antero Midstream finishing near the high end of its updated guidance for 2025 and having potential to generate $1.3 billion in adjusted EBITDA in 2027.

This valuation estimate also assumes a 50/50 chance that it prevails in the Veolia lawsuit. If it wins $300+ million in the end, I would increase its estimated value to $21.50 per share.

Antero Midstream's current share price is above $21, so I'm moving back to a "Neutral" outlook on it.

Conclusion

Antero Midstream finished 2025 with adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow after dividends that were around the high end of its revised guidance. It also expects some improvements in 2026 that are being driven by its HG Energy acquisition (although I believe its 2026 guidance is conservative).

Antero Midstream expects solid adjusted EBITDA growth in 2027, which could push its adjusted EBITDA up to $1.3 billion (or more). This should be enough to support an increased quarterly dividend of $0.30 per share.

Antero Midstream's estimated value is now $21.20 per share, up $1 from my last estimate (after the HG Energy acquisition announcement). This is close to Antero Midstream's current share price after its significant gains in recent days.

data.title: AI Is Not My Biggest Concern With Stifel Financial (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
This article serves as an update to my previous analysis of Stifel Financial (SF) that was written in May of 2025. At that time, the company had just reported results from Q1 2025 that included a big shortfall on earnings due to elevated provisions for legal matters. Stifel had been ordered to pay $132.5 million after an arbitration panel determined the company misrepresented the risk involved for an investment of structured notes. My opinion of SF at that time was that the stock was worth buying in spite of this noise because of its strong position in the wealth management industry. Since my article was published, Stifel has gained 41.5%, over 19% more than the S&P 500.

As I write this follow-up today, things seem similar to where they were last May, but for different reasons. Over the last week, SF has taken a hit of over 8% for another concern, the creeping presence of artificial intelligence in the wealth management space. Privately held financial software company Altruist unveiled a new use for its AI platform known as “Hazel” that can create personalized tax strategies for clients “within minutes”. The high-net-worth financial services sector is just the latest to be impacted by concerns that AI will diminish future profit growth for traditional businesses.

I am not convinced that the sky is falling for SF and its peers, but I think the recent selloff for the stock may be justified for other reasons. The legal issues that were problematic for the company at the start of last year are not going away, and the valuation of its stock has become a little stretched. If the stock were to drop another 8 to 10% in the weeks ahead, I could be swayed to return to my Buy rating, but for now, I am updating my opinion of Stifel Financial to a Hold.

Company Overview

Stifel Financial Corporation defines itself as a diversified financial services holding company. It is perhaps best known for its brokerage and investment banking subsidiary, Stifel, Nicolaus & Company. SF has over half a trillion dollars in client assets under management, and the company has stated a public goal of reaching $1 trillion with no timeline set for that achievement. For a more detailed description of the company’s operations and history, readers are encouraged to look back at my article from last May.

SF currently has a market cap of $12.6 billion. In FY 2025, it collected over $5.5 billion in revenues, which was a record for the company. Global wealth management contributed 64.0% of that total. Stifel’s institutional operations, which include merger advisory and underwriting services, contributed the bulk of the remaining revenue at 34.6%. A more detailed report of the company’s financial results from Q4 2025 as well as the full year can be seen below.

SF currently has a forward yield of 1.63%. On January 27, the company announced that it was raising its quarterly dividend by 10.9% to $0.51 per share. Barring a decrease in the payout in the final three quarters of the year, 2025 will be the ninth straight year that the company has increased its total yearly distribution. While its overall yield is below the average for its sector, SF's five-year compound average dividend growth rate is over 32%.

Analysis of Stifel Financial

On January 28, Stifel Financial published its financial results from Q4 2025. Revenues in the quarter were $1.56 billion, an increase of 14.7% from the same quarter in 2024. Non-GAAP net income of $2.63 per share topped expectations by $0.12.

When all four quarters from 2025 are combined, Stifel put together a banner year, at least from a revenue perspective. The company produced all-time highs in net revenue, global wealth management revenue, and asset management revenue. SF also announced a three-for-two stock split that will become effective on February 27. The market tended to take most of this news in stride as SF fell by almost 0.1% on the day this news was released.

SF had a more attractive valuation when I first wrote about the company last May. At that time, SF traded with a trailing PE ratio of 14.8. Its PE for FY 2025 is now 18.6. The last time the stock traded at that high of a valuation was back in early 2018. Earnings growth for this year is projected to be just over 44%, however, making a higher multiple more reasonable. The forward PE for Stifel for FY 2026 is just 12.9.

When discussing the results in the Q4 2025 earnings release, Stifel Chairman and CEO Ronald Kruszewski said last year “marked a record year for Stifel and demonstrated the strength of [its] platform and long-term strategy.” He added, “While we remain attentive to market and geopolitical risks, we are confident in our ability to navigate uncertainty and continue to deliver for clients and shareholders.”

Yet, not everything from the last year was positive for the company from a trend perspective. SF's compensation expense ratio rose from 58.7% in FY 2024 to 59.2% last year. GAAP earnings for the full year actually declined when compared to the previous year by just over 6% to $5.87 per share. Return on common equity and return on tangible common equity, two non-GAAP measures reported by the company, dropped by 1.1% and 1.7%, respectively, due largely to elevated legal provisions.

Looking ahead to FY 2026, Stifel Financial expects revenues to rise 12%, from $5.5 billion to $6.2 billion. The compensation ratio, with non-GAAP calculations, is expected to drop 60 basis points. Non-GAAP earnings are expected to rise 22%. (Non-GAAP earnings do not take out provisions for legal matters.)

Risks to Consider

The legal issues that were publicized at the start of 2025 have not gone away. A recent appeal of the $132.5 million lawsuit was denied. Stifel has until February 20 to object to the recommendations from the most recent court decision. Just one month ago, Stifel paid out an $850,000 settlement to settle a related case. The company faces a total of 23 pending cases, all related to the same former employee. Stifel’s current running total of arbitration awards and settlements is now almost $182 million. There is an additional lawsuit pending over excessive fees and poor services for 401(k) advisement that was filed last July. I can find no reputable current estimate of what that case could cost the company. I expect these matters to be a cloud that hovers over the company for at least the first half of this year.

Stifel’s investment newsletter that was published last month, outlined several potential threats to the overall economy both here in the US and abroad. Higher levels of market expansion and economic activity are a needed tailwind for Stifel’s business operations. Included in this newsletter were the following threats for 2026:

  • Instability caused by the midterm elections in the US
  • Rising debt levels in the US that reduce confidence in US treasuries and the US dollar
  • Inflation staying well above the Fed’s 2% target
  • Unknown impact from changes in leadership at the Federal Reserve
  • Disruption caused by artificial intelligence

Any number of these concerns could make it challenging for Stifel to meet its projections for top and bottom-line growth in the year ahead as trading volumes and fee generation is suppressed. The loan portfolio controlled by Stifel's banking operations could face higher default rates with a significant economic slowdown.

Yes, artificial intelligence is a potential threat to the future for Stifel Financial and its peers, but I am not as concerned about this at the current time. There is no reason that SF cannot use its own AI tools to assist clients in the future. As AI improves efficiencies in other areas, Stifel expects it will be an amplifier, not a detractor, for its earnings. The company may have to increase some expenses to stay current with the latest innovations in its sector, but I list this risk last on my list for a reason, as I see it as the smallest from the ones listed here. So much of what the company does on a daily basis goes beyond creating tax strategies. At least for now, Stifel's business is still very much relationship driven, especially for activities like research and M&A. These activities require trust and judgment that cannot be fully replicated by AI, at least, not yet.

The Bottom Line

When everything is considered together, I do think that Stifel Financial could be set up for another solid performance in 2026. However, when I rated the stock as a Buy previously, I was working with the belief that it would outperform the market. Now, I believe its performance will be more in line with its peers. This year could be a strong year for the financial sector overall, so exposure to the investment banking and brokerage sector may pay off. However, investors can buy stock in a peer company like Moelis and Company (MC) to get the same exposure without the risk of the lingering legal matters.

Stifel Financial is considered a Hold by Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating scale. The company’s lowest score is in valuation, stemming from the company’s high PE ratio for the last year when compared to peers from its sector. Stifel’s Profitability score is also impacted by the company’s margins, which are lower than the sector median as well as its own five-year average for that metric.

In downgrading SF to a Hold, I am not suggesting that the wheels are completely falling off for the company's operations. I just do not think the stock represents the same potential bargain that it did last May.

data.title: FCT: No Near-Term Growth Catalyst
data.description:
Overview

With market indices pulling back from their all-time highs, some investors may be looking for a safe haven. First Trust Senior Floating Rate Income Fund II (FCT) provides exposure to a diverse range of income-producing debt securities, which can offer stability that is disconnected from traditional equities. When I previously covered FCT, I issued a hold rating due to the limited growth potential due to high interest rates at the time. Since my last coverage, the interest rate environment has shifted, and the fund has released an updated semi-annual report for 2026, which prompted me to revisit the fund's value proposition now that we've crossed into the new year.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that FCT's share price has declined by about 4.6%. The fund has failed to participate in the market rally over the last few months but still remains resilient during this period of elevated interest rates. When including all distributions that were paid to shareholders, the total return jumps up to nearly 7.2% over the same time frame. FCT now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 11.7% while issuing payouts on a monthly basis. However, I remain cautious about the overall sustainability of the current payouts if performance does not improve.

Despite the fund's suppressed share price over the last few years, FCT still trades at the higher end of its price to NAV range. Therefore, valuation can be a bit tricky here, but I don't believe that this is the time to accumulate yet. Additionally, the fund's NAV growth has been limited since its earnings haven't substantially improved yet. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that FCT implements to generate its earnings for shareholders.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, FCT has total managed assets of $292.7M that are spread across a diverse range of income-producing securities. The fund has a listed expense ratio of 2.42%, but this is a bit misleading since it includes the fund's costs related to interest expenses and other operating fees. Since FCT is structured as a closed-end fund, it is legally required to bulk in these costs as part of its expense ratio. When excluding these other costs, the actual management fee sits at 0.92% of net assets, which is the actual cost that investors see.

The fund has a primary goal of generating a high current income so that it can produce a high rate of distributions. In order to achieve this goal, FCT aims to allocate at least 80% of its assets to senior loans. This is ideal because senior secured loans sit at the top of the corporate capital structure and have the highest priority for repayment, which protects the fund from losing all invested capital in a bad investment. The breakdown by investment type is as follows:

  • Senior Floating-Rate Loan Interests: ~96.72%
  • Corporate Bonds and Notes: ~2.13%
  • Foreign Corporate Bonds and Notes: ~1.01%

Expanding on this, FCT also aims to maintain an efficient level of diversity across industries to help mitigate any sort of concentration risks. As we can see below, FCT is most heavily allocated to insurance securities at 16.39% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to software and health care providers, which amounts to 15.02% and 7.94% of assets, respectively.

I like the heavy allocation to insurance-based securities because these are businesses that typically have consistent cash flows and low turnover. So this is an industry that may be solid for debt securities since they can offer stability during uncertain market environments. Similarly, the allocation to software securities can also be great because these tend to be subscription-based businesses that offer stable cash flows and may be more resilient to shifts in the technology market.

Lastly, the fund provides a ton of exposure to private entities. This expands the level of diversity within the fund and can be a solid introduction for investors that want to obtain a hedge against the public equities market. However, we should remember that FCT maintains exposure to these positions through debt securities, so the fund may not be able to directly participate in the growth of its own holdings.

Valuation & Performance Insights

At the time of my last coverage, FCT traded at a discount to NAV of 3.74%. Despite the share price declining in recent months, FCT now trades at a slightly smaller discount to NAV of 1.20%. For reference, the fund has traded at an average discount to NAV of 5.89% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that FCT now trades at the higher end of its historical price-to-NAV range over the last ten years. Therefore, I believe that it is still not a good time to accumulate shares.

Since my last coverage, FCT released an updated semi-annual report for 2026. This is the most updated report that provides transparency into the fund's earnings. Net investment income amounted to $0.36 per share, while net realized gains came in flat. Looking back at prior years, FCT is aligned to underperform its typical range for net investment income, assuming a similar performance over the remainder of the period. This could be an indication that the portfolio experienced some unfavorable shifts or the direct impact from lower interest rates.

Since the time of my last coverage, the Fed initiated three separate 25 basis point cuts to interest rates throughout 2025. Since the portfolio is primarily comprised of floating rate debt securities, a lower interest rate can have a direct impact on the net investment income that the fund captures. Assuming that interest rates continue to trend lower over the next twelve months, we may see net investment income also trend lower. Unless management is capable of allocating capital towards new investment activity to offset these losses, I believe there's a good chance that we'll see earnings trend lower in the near term.

One of the issues is that net investment income doesn't sufficiently cover the distributions that are being paid. Therefore, FCT is reliant on positive net realized gains in order to bridge that gap in its earnings. The issue is that net realized gains can wildly vary based on market conditions, and this makes it hard to accurately predict how the underlying NAV will perform over time. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts over the last few years:

  • 2021: $12.70 per share
  • 2022: $11.30 per share
  • 2023: $10.96 per share
  • 2024: $11.03 per share
  • 2025: $10.58 per share
  • 2026 (semi-annual): $10.36 per share

Since the fund is dependent on net realized gains, FCT may find itself in a position where it is paying out more than it actually earns sometimes. This is most likely to occur during periods of market declines, which is when FCT may see net realized losses. So while market indices have continued to trade at all-time highs, FCT is not structurally capable of participating in this growth. As we can see below, FCT's share price has declined by 16.8% over the last five years, which severely underperforms against the S&P 500's (SPY) price growth of 73.5% over the same time frame. Even when including all distributions that were paid out, FCT still severely underperforms a more standard index-tracking ETF.

Dividend Risk

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.097 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11.7%. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $1.164 per share. Referring to the same semi-annual report, FCT's actual dividend coverage remains very inconsistent. Since 2021, FCT has only been able to generate sufficient earnings in two of the last five years. If interest rates continue to trend downward and FCT's earnings worsen, I think there is a solid chance that distributions will be cut over the next twelve months.

For this reason, it is hard to recommend a position in FCT for income investors at this time. The whole reason that investors maintain exposure to a high-yielding fund like this is for the income that it can provide. If that income is under the threat of reduction, it defeats the whole purpose of maintaining a position. This is just the nature of floating rate funds, so FCT may not be the best buy-and-hold position for income investors seeking stability.

Lastly, investors should consider the risk that the distributions received from FCT do not offer much tax-efficiency. For instance, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that the majority of the payouts issued YTD were classified as net investment income, which has the greatest tax burden for investors. Therefore, a position in FCT would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to limit their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that FCT remains a hold at this time. In an environment where interest rates continue to trend lower, I don't believe there are any near-term catalysts that can help the fund's underlying earnings improve. A lower interest rate environment will translate to lower net investment income. Additionally, I believe that the lower earnings will threaten the current distributions. Therefore, I anticipate that payouts may be cut in the near-term if earnings are not improved. Lastly, the fund trades at one of the least attractive price-to-NAV valuations over the last decade, which further reinforces that this is not an ideal time for accumulation.
